skill.name: xss-html-injection
skill.description: Guides authorized XSS and HTML injection testing, including detection workflows, payload context analysis, exploit impact demonstration, and remediation guidance.
skill.summary: Test authorized web applications for XSS and HTML injection risks.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static script and network findings are mostly true positives as Markdown payload examples, not executable skill code. However, the skill explicitly teaches session hijacking, credential theft, keylogging, phishing form injection, storage capture, obfuscation, and CSP bypass, so it is high risk and not safe to publish without major restriction.

high_findings.title: Credential and Session Exfiltration Payloads
high_findings.description: The skill provides payload examples that send cookies, localStorage, page URLs, and session data to attacker-controlled endpoints. These examples are framed as exploitation techniques and can directly enable credential theft if used outside an authorized lab.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited sections explicitly combine browser credential sources with outbound requests to attacker endpoints. Although the content is Markdown, the semantic intent and copy-ready payload structure are clear.

high_findings.title: Keylogging and Phishing Form Injection Guidance
high_findings.description: The skill includes a keylogger injection payload and fake login form examples that collect credentials through injected HTML. This crosses from vulnerability detection into credential capture workflows.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples are labeled as keylogger, phishing, form hijacking, and iframe injection. The surrounding text describes exploitation, making the harmful use case explicit.

high_findings.title: Filter Bypass and Obfuscation Techniques
high_findings.description: The skill teaches case changes, malformed tags, Unicode and entity encoding, dynamic code execution, constructor execution, base64 decoding, and CSP bypass. These techniques help evade defenses rather than only validate a vulnerability.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited sections are structured as bypass guidance and include dynamic execution and obfuscation examples. Legitimate testing is possible, but the material materially improves exploit reliability.

medium_findings.title: Dangerous DOM Sink Inventory
medium_findings.description: The skill lists document.write, innerHTML, insertAdjacentHTML, eval, Function, and location assignment as DOM XSS sinks. This is useful for auditing, but it also supports exploit construction when combined with the payload sections.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sink list is accurate security education, and the later innerHTML example confirms exploit context. The risk is moderate because the list alone is dual-use.

medium_findings.title: External Command Static Findings Are False Positives
medium_findings.description: The many Ruby or shell backtick findings are caused by Markdown code fences and inline formatting, not executable shell commands. No evidence found of actual command execution by the skill file.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are documentation examples inside SKILL.md. The repository contains only this Markdown skill file, so there is no executable command path in the skill package.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Attacker URLs in Examples
low_findings.description: The hardcoded attacker.com and example target URLs are documentation placeholders, but they normalize external collection endpoints throughout the skill. This is a low standalone risk and a high contextual risk when combined with credential payloads.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are clearly example domains in Markdown. They still support the confirmed exfiltration and phishing workflows described elsewhere.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Static Finding Is Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The hidden file access finding at line 332 appears to be a parser artifact from a JavaScript regular expression example. No evidence found of filesystem access or hidden file reads.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is inside an XSS payload example, not a file API call. The skill package has no supporting script that could read local files.

low_findings.title: Keyword-Only Static Findings Are Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: Several weak cryptography, certificate file, browser credential file, and reconnaissance detections appear to be keyword matches inside prose or payload examples. No evidence found of actual cryptographic use, certificate file access, browser profile file reads, or reconnaissance tooling.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are Markdown text, checklist items, or JavaScript event examples. They do not show executable filesystem, cryptographic, or reconnaissance operations.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dynamic Browser Code Execution Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill demonstrates eval, Function constructor, and base64-decoded execution as XSS obfuscation methods.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: These patterns are directly present and labeled as JavaScript obfuscation or dangerous sinks. They are not executed by the skill itself.

dangerous_patterns.title: Browser Credential Source Collection
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill demonstrates collection of document.cookie and localStorage followed by outbound transmission.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited payloads explicitly read browser credential stores and send them to external endpoints. This is strong evidence of high-risk instructional content.

dangerous_patterns.title: Injected Credential Capture UI
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill shows fake login and form hijacking content that captures user-entered credentials.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples are labeled as phishing and form hijacking. The forms post credential-like fields to attacker-controlled endpoints.

content.user_title: Assess XSS and HTML Injection Risks
content.value_statement: Client-side injection flaws can expose sessions, stored data, and user trust. This skill organizes authorized XSS testing, impact validation, and remediation planning.
actual_capabilities: Maps reflected, stored, and DOM-based XSS testing workflows.
Identifies common input sources and dangerous DOM sinks.
Explains how to validate impact in authorized test environments.
Documents filter bypass concepts and encoding variations.
Provides remediation themes such as output encoding and CSP review.
Frames findings for vulnerability reports and severity discussion.
limitations: Contains offensive payload examples that require strict authorization.
Does not run automated scanning or verify findings independently.
Does not replace legal approval, scoping, or professional review.
Some examples are unsafe for production systems without containment.

use_cases.title: Validate a Bug Bounty Finding
use_cases.description: Confirm the XSS type, document impact, and prepare a clear report within program scope.

use_cases.title: Review a Web Feature Before Release
use_cases.description: Check user-controlled fields, DOM sinks, and encoding behavior before shipping a new workflow.

use_cases.title: Plan Developer Remediation
use_cases.description: Translate confirmed injection behavior into practical fixes, test cases, and CSP improvements.

prompt_templates.title: Start an Authorized XSS Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me plan an authorized XSS and HTML injection review for this application area. Keep testing non-destructive and within scope.

prompt_templates.title: Classify an Injection Finding
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this observed reflection behavior and help classify whether it is stored, reflected, DOM-based, or not exploitable.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Safe Impact Narrative
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me write a responsible vulnerability report that explains impact without including credential theft steps.

prompt_templates.title: Design Remediation Tests
prompt_templates.prompt: Given this confirmed XSS class and affected context, propose output encoding, sanitization, CSP, and regression tests.

output_examples.input: A search page reflects a query parameter into page text without encoding.
output_examples.output: The output classifies the issue as reflected XSS risk, lists safe validation steps, explains user impact, and recommends contextual output encoding.

output_examples.input: A profile bio supports limited HTML and strips script tags.
output_examples.output: The output identifies stored HTML injection concerns, suggests allowlist sanitization, and recommends tests for attributes, links, and event handlers.

output_examples.input: A dashboard reads the URL fragment and writes it into the page.
output_examples.output: The output flags DOM-based XSS risk, traces the source-to-sink path, and proposes safer DOM APIs and regression coverage.

best_practices: Use the skill only with written authorization and a defined testing scope.
Prefer harmless proof of execution and avoid collecting real credentials or sessions.
Pair every confirmed issue with remediation guidance and regression tests.
anti_patterns: Do not test systems where you lack explicit permission.
Do not deploy payloads that persist, spread, or capture real user data.
Do not publish exploit details before the owner has time to remediate.

faq.question: Is this skill safe for general marketplace use?
faq.answer: No. It contains high-risk offensive examples and should require strong restrictions before publication.

faq.question: Does the skill execute code by itself?
faq.answer: No. The reviewed package is a Markdown skill file, but it includes copy-ready exploit examples.

faq.question: Can it help with defensive testing?
faq.answer: Yes. It can guide authorized validation, classification, reporting, and remediation planning.

faq.question: What authorization is required?
faq.answer: Written approval, clear target scope, data handling rules, and escalation procedures are required.

faq.question: Should real session data be captured?
faq.answer: No. Use harmless demonstrations and avoid collecting real user credentials, cookies, or storage data.

faq.question: What should be changed before publishing?
faq.answer: Remove credential theft, keylogging, phishing, and bypass payloads, then focus on safe detection and remediation.
